questions.q: Under ASC 606, how should Welk Company account for a sale of goods for which no over-time criterion is met?
choices: Recognize revenue at the point in time control transfers
Recognize revenue over time as costs are incurred
Defer all revenue until cash is collected
Recognize revenue when the contract is signed
questions.explain: When none of the three over-time criteria are met, the obligation is satisfied at a point in time, so recognize revenue when control transfers. Indicators of control transfer: physical possession, legal title, risks and rewards, customer acceptance, and a present right to payment. Cash collection and contract signing are not, by themselves, control-transfer events.

questions.q: Under ASC 606, how should Marr Company account for a service the customer simultaneously receives and consumes as the entity performs?
choices: Recognize revenue at completion
Recognize revenue over time
Recognize revenue when invoiced
Defer until the customer confirms satisfaction
questions.explain: This is the first over-time criterion: the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits as the entity performs (routine or recurring services such as cleaning or transaction processing). Recognize revenue over time using a measure of progress. Invoice date and completion date do not drive recognition here.

questions.q: Under ASC 606, how should Oste Company account for construction of an asset the customer controls as it is built?
choices: Recognize revenue at delivery
Recognize revenue when title transfers
Recognize revenue over time
Recognize revenue only upon final inspection
questions.explain: Second over-time criterion: the customer controls the asset as it is created or enhanced (for example, building on land the customer owns). Recognize revenue over time by a measure of progress. Title transfer or final inspection is not the trigger when the customer already controls the work in progress.

questions.q: Under ASC 606, how should Penn Company account for an asset with no alternative use to the entity plus an enforceable right to payment for performance to date?
choices: Recognize revenue at the point of delivery
Defer revenue until the asset is sold to a third party
Recognize revenue when production begins
Recognize revenue over time
questions.explain: Third over-time criterion: the asset has no alternative use to the seller AND the seller has an enforceable right to payment for performance completed to date. Both conditions must hold. When they do, recognize revenue over time using a progress measure, not at delivery and not when production merely begins.

questions.q: Under ASC 606, how should Quade Company account for variable consideration such as a performance bonus or rebate?
choices: Estimate it and include only the amount highly probable not to reverse
Include the full amount regardless of reversal risk
Exclude it entirely until it is received
Recognize it only when the cash is collected
questions.explain: Estimate variable consideration using the expected value or the most-likely-amount method, then apply the constraint: include it only to the extent it is highly probable that a significant revenue reversal will not occur. Including the full amount ignores the constraint; excluding it entirely until cash arrives understates revenue when a reliable estimate exists.

questions.q: Under ASC 606, how should Tarn Company account for an arrangement where the entity arranges for a third party to provide the good and does not control it before transfer?
choices: Recognize revenue gross, as a principal
Recognize revenue net, as an agent
Recognize no revenue at all
Recognize revenue equal to the amount paid to the supplier
questions.explain: Control before transfer is the deciding test. If the entity only arranges for another party to provide the good or service and does not control it before transfer, it is an agent and recognizes only the net fee or commission. A principal, which controls the good first, recognizes the gross amount.

questions.q: Under ASC 606, how should Volk Company account for sales taxes collected from customers on behalf of the government?
choices: Include them in revenue
Recognize them as other income
Exclude them from the transaction price
Defer them as a contract liability
questions.explain: Amounts collected on behalf of third parties, such as sales and use taxes remitted to the government, are not the entity's consideration, so exclude them from the transaction price and from revenue. They are a payable to the taxing authority, not other income and not a contract liability.

questions.q: Under ASC 606, how should Wren Company account for goods shipped to a consignee under a consignment arrangement?
choices: Recognize revenue when the goods are shipped to the consignee
Recognize revenue when the consignee receives the goods
Recognize revenue ratably over the consignment period
Recognize no revenue until the consignee sells to an end customer
questions.explain: In a consignment the consignor keeps control until the consignee sells to a third party, so shipping to the consignee is not a sale. Recognize revenue (and derecognize inventory) only when the end customer buys. Consignment indicators: the product is returnable, the consignor can require its return or transfer, and the consignee has no unconditional obligation to pay for it.

questions.q: Under ASC 606, how should Yale Company account for a bill-and-hold arrangement?
choices: Recognize revenue only if the strict bill-and-hold criteria are met
Recognize revenue when payment is received
Never recognize revenue until physical delivery
Recognize revenue when the order is placed
questions.explain: Bill-and-hold can recognize revenue before shipment only if ALL four criteria hold: the reason is substantive (e.g., the customer asked for it), the goods are separately identified as the customer's, they are ready for physical transfer, and the seller cannot use them or redirect them to another customer. Otherwise control has not transferred. Receiving payment or taking the order does not transfer control, and a blanket 'never until delivery' rule ignores the exception.

questions.q: Under ASC 606, how should Zorn Company account for a contract that fails the 'probable collection' criterion?
choices: Recognize revenue and a large allowance
Conclude no contract exists and recognize no revenue
Recognize revenue over the expected collection period
Recognize revenue net of estimated bad debts
questions.explain: Collectibility is a Step 1 gate in ASC 606. If it is not probable the entity will collect the consideration it is entitled to, no contract exists yet, so no revenue is recognized; any cash received sits as a deposit/liability until the criteria are met or the cash is nonrefundable and performance is done. You do not gross up revenue with an allowance, that conflates a Step 1 failure with ordinary bad-debt estimation.

questions.q: Under ASC 606, how should Ames Company account for allocation of a bundled transaction price among multiple performance obligations?
choices: Allocate equally to each obligation
Allocate entirely to the obligation delivered first
Allocate based on relative standalone selling prices
Allocate based on the entity's cost of each obligation
questions.explain: Step 4 allocates the transaction price to each performance obligation in proportion to its standalone selling price (SSP). When SSP is not directly observable, estimate it (adjusted market assessment, expected cost plus a margin, or the residual approach). Equal splits and cost-based splits are not permitted, and you do not load the entire price onto whatever is delivered first.

questions.q: Under ASC 606, how should Birk Company account for a product sold with a right of return?
choices: Recognize the full sales amount with no adjustment
Defer all revenue until the return period expires
Recognize revenue only for items not returned, when known
Recognize revenue net of expected returns, with a refund liability and return asset
questions.explain: A right of return is variable consideration. Recognize revenue only for the amount you expect to keep, set up a refund liability for expected returns, and record a return asset (the former carrying amount of goods, less expected recovery cost). Recognizing the full sale ignores the variability; deferring everything understates revenue when returns are reasonably estimable.

questions.q: For Pare Company, a lessee, a lease in which ownership of the asset transfers to the lessee by the end of the term is classified as:
choices: A finance lease
An operating lease
A short-term lease expensed straight-line
A direct-financing lease
questions.explain: Transfer of ownership to the lessee by the end of the lease term is the first finance-lease criterion. Meeting any single criterion makes it a finance lease (front-loaded interest plus separate amortization of the right-of-use asset), not an operating lease.

questions.q: For Shel Company, a lessee, a lease in which the lease contains a purchase option the lessee is reasonably certain to exercise is classified as:
choices: An operating lease
A finance lease
A short-term lease
A service contract
questions.explain: A purchase option the lessee is reasonably certain to exercise is the second finance-lease criterion, so the lease is a finance lease. 'Reasonably certain' is a high threshold driven by economic incentive (such as a bargain price). It is not an operating or short-term lease, and a lease conveying control of an identified asset is not a mere service contract.

questions.q: For Welk Company, a lessee, a lease in which the lease term is for the major part of the asset's remaining economic life is classified as:
choices: An operating lease
A short-term lease
A finance lease
An executory contract
questions.explain: The third criterion is that the lease term is for the major part of the asset's remaining economic life (commonly assessed around a 75% guideline). Meeting it makes the lease a finance lease, not an operating or executory contract.

questions.q: For Marr Company, a lessee, a lease in which the present value of the payments is substantially all of the asset's fair value is classified as:
choices: An operating lease
A short-term lease
A month-to-month lease
A finance lease
questions.explain: The fourth criterion is that the present value of the lease payments, plus any residual value the lessee guarantees, equals substantially all of the asset's fair value (commonly around 90% or more). Meeting it classifies the lease as a finance lease.

questions.q: For Oste Company, a lessee, a lease in which the asset is so specialized it has no alternative use to the lessor at the end of the term is classified as:
choices: A finance lease
An operating lease
A short-term lease
A sale-leaseback
questions.explain: The fifth criterion is that the underlying asset is so specialized it has no alternative use to the lessor at the end of the term. Meeting it classifies the lease as a finance lease.

questions.q: For Penn Company, a lessee, a lease in which a lease in which none of the five finance-lease criteria are met is classified as:
choices: A finance lease
An operating lease
A sales-type lease
A direct-financing lease
questions.explain: A lessee classifies a lease as a finance lease if ANY one of five criteria is met: transfer of ownership, a purchase option reasonably certain to be exercised, lease term covering the major part of the asset's remaining economic life, present value of payments equal to substantially all of fair value, or an asset so specialized it has no alternative use to the lessor. If none are met, it is an operating lease, still on balance sheet as a right-of-use asset and lease liability, but with a single straight-line lease expense.

questions.q: For Quade Company, a lessee, a lease in which a lease of 12 months or less with no purchase option is classified as:
choices: Always a finance lease
Always capitalized at present value
Eligible for the short-term lease election (expense payments straight-line)
Never recognized in the financial statements
questions.explain: A lease of 12 months or less with no purchase option the lessee is reasonably certain to exercise qualifies for the short-term lease policy election: skip the right-of-use asset and lease liability and recognize the payments straight-line over the term. It is not automatically a finance lease, and it is still reflected in expense.

questions.q: For Hale Company, a lessee, a lease in which a lease in which none of the five finance-lease criteria are met is classified as:
choices: A finance lease
A sales-type lease
A direct-financing lease
An operating lease
questions.explain: A lessee classifies a lease as a finance lease if ANY one of five criteria is met: transfer of ownership, a purchase option reasonably certain to be exercised, lease term covering the major part of the asset's remaining economic life, present value of payments equal to substantially all of fair value, or an asset so specialized it has no alternative use to the lessor. If none are met, it is an operating lease, still on balance sheet as a right-of-use asset and lease liability, but with a single straight-line lease expense.

questions.q: For Ives Company, a lessee, a lease in which a lease of 12 months or less with no purchase option is classified as:
choices: Eligible for the short-term lease election (expense payments straight-line)
Always a finance lease
Always capitalized at present value
Never recognized in the financial statements
questions.explain: A lease of 12 months or less with no purchase option the lessee is reasonably certain to exercise qualifies for the short-term lease policy election: skip the right-of-use asset and lease liability and recognize the payments straight-line over the term. It is not automatically a finance lease, and it is still reflected in expense.

questions.q: In preparing consolidated financial statements for Jode Company and its subsidiary, intercompany receivables and payables between a parent and subsidiary is:
choices: Eliminated only to the extent of the parent's ownership
Eliminated in full, regardless of the ownership percentage
Reported as related-party balances
Netted against noncontrolling interest
questions.explain: Intercompany receivables and payables are eliminated in full in consolidation, even when the subsidiary is only partially owned, because the consolidated entity cannot owe money to itself. The noncontrolling-interest percentage affects the allocation of equity and income, not whether intra-group balances are removed.

questions.q: In preparing consolidated financial statements for Kerr Company and its subsidiary, the noncontrolling interest at the acquisition date under U.S. GAAP is:
choices: Measured at the parent's cost
Measured at the NCI's share of book value
Measured at fair value
Not recognized until the subsidiary is sold
questions.explain: Under U.S. GAAP, noncontrolling interest is measured at fair value on the acquisition date (the full-goodwill approach), and it is recognized immediately as a component of consolidated equity. It is not the parent's cost and not merely the NCI's share of book value.

questions.q: In preparing consolidated financial statements for Lund Company and its subsidiary, acquisition-related legal, advisory, and due-diligence costs is:
choices: Capitalized as part of the consideration transferred
Added to goodwill
Recorded as a reduction of equity
Expensed as incurred
questions.explain: Under the acquisition method, direct acquisition costs (legal, advisory, due-diligence) are expensed as incurred. They are not part of consideration transferred and do not affect goodwill. (Costs to issue debt or equity are handled under their own guidance, separate from this rule.)

questions.q: In preparing consolidated financial statements for Mabe Company and its subsidiary, the accounting method required for a business combination is:
choices: The acquisition method
The pooling-of-interests method
The equity method
The proportionate consolidation method
questions.explain: Under U.S. GAAP, noncontrolling interest is measured at fair value on the acquisition date (the full-goodwill approach), and it is recognized immediately as a component of consolidated equity. It is not the parent's cost and not merely the NCI's share of book value.

questions.q: In preparing consolidated financial statements for Nuss Company and its subsidiary, unrealized profit in ending inventory from an intercompany sale is:
choices: Recognized immediately in consolidated income
Deferred until the inventory is sold to an outside party
Allocated entirely to noncontrolling interest
Ignored if the subsidiary is wholly owned
questions.explain: Profit on intercompany inventory still on hand at year-end is unrealized to the group, so defer it until that inventory is sold to an outside party. It is not recognized immediately, and for upstream (subsidiary-to-parent) sales the deferral is shared between the controlling and noncontrolling interests.

questions.q: In preparing consolidated financial statements for Pace Company and its subsidiary, a gain on an intercompany sale of land is:
choices: Recognized in consolidated income in the year of sale
Recorded directly in equity
Deferred until the land is sold outside the consolidated group
Amortized over the land's remaining life
questions.explain: Intercompany profit is not earned from the consolidated group's perspective until the asset leaves the group. A gain on an intercompany land sale is eliminated and deferred, then recognized only when the land is sold to an outside party. It is not recognized in the year of the intercompany sale, and it is never recorded directly in equity.

questions.q: In preparing consolidated financial statements for Rune Company and its subsidiary, an investment that conveys control (more than 50% of voting interest) is:
choices: Accounted for under the equity method
Reported at fair value through net income
Reported at amortized cost
Consolidated
questions.explain: A controlling financial interest, generally more than 50% of voting interest absent other arrangements, requires consolidation: combine 100% of the subsidiary's assets, liabilities, revenues, and expenses, and present any noncontrolling interest within equity. The equity method (significant influence, roughly 20-50%) and fair-value or amortized-cost reporting do not apply once control exists.

questions.q: In preparing consolidated financial statements for Selk Company and its subsidiary, goodwill recognized in a business combination is:
choices: Consideration transferred + fair value of NCI − fair value of identifiable net assets
Consideration transferred − book value of net assets
The excess of book value over fair value of net assets
Consideration transferred + fair value of identifiable net assets
questions.explain: Goodwill equals consideration transferred plus the fair value of any noncontrolling interest plus the fair value of any previously held equity interest, minus the fair value of identifiable net assets acquired. It is a residual measured against fair values, never book values.

questions.q: In governmental fund accounting, revenue recognition under the modified accrual basis is:
choices: When earned, regardless of collection
When measurable and available
When the budget is adopted
When cash is ultimately received, always
questions.explain: Under modified accrual, governmental-fund revenue is recognized when it is both measurable and available, meaning collectible within the period or soon enough after year-end to pay current-period obligations. 'When earned regardless of collection' describes full accrual, not modified accrual.

questions.q: In governmental fund accounting, the 'available' criterion for property taxes is:
choices: Collectible within the next fiscal year
Collectible at any future date
Collectible within 60 days after year-end
Collectible within 90 days after year-end
questions.explain: For property taxes under modified accrual, 'available' generally means collectible within 60 days after year-end. Amounts that do not meet the availability window are reported as a deferred inflow of resources rather than revenue.

questions.q: In governmental fund accounting, a capital asset purchased by a governmental fund is:
choices: Capitalized and depreciated in the fund
Reported as an other financing use
Recorded only in fiduciary funds
Reported as an expenditure of the fund (not capitalized in the fund)
questions.explain: In a governmental fund (current financial resources focus, modified accrual), purchasing a capital asset is recorded as an expenditure; the asset is not capitalized or depreciated within the fund. It is capitalized and depreciated only in the government-wide statements.

questions.q: In governmental fund accounting, long-term debt issued by a governmental fund is:
choices: Recorded as an other financing source (the liability appears only government-wide)
Recorded as a fund liability
Recorded as fund revenue
Netted against capital outlay
questions.explain: Issuing long-term debt in a governmental fund is recorded as an other financing source, because it provides current financial resources. The long-term liability itself appears only in the government-wide statements, not as a fund liability.

questions.q: In governmental fund accounting, the fund balance categories, most-constrained to least is:
choices: Restricted, Unrestricted, Designated, Reserved, Free
Nonspendable, Restricted, Committed, Assigned, Unassigned
Reserved, Designated, Unreserved
Spendable, Nonspendable, Reserved, Free
questions.explain: GASB 54 fund-balance categories, most-constrained to least, are Nonspendable, Restricted, Committed, Assigned, and Unassigned. The older reserved/designated/unreserved labels are obsolete.

questions.q: In governmental fund accounting, when encumbrances are recorded is:
choices: When the goods are received
When the invoice is paid
When a purchase commitment (such as a purchase order) is made
At the end of the fiscal year only
questions.explain: An encumbrance is recorded when a purchase commitment is made, such as when a purchase order is issued, reserving budgetary appropriation. It is reversed when the actual expenditure is recorded upon receipt, not when the invoice is paid and not only at year-end.

questions.q: In governmental fund accounting, the measurement focus and basis used by the government-wide statements is:
choices: Current financial resources focus, modified accrual
Cash basis
Budgetary basis
Economic resources focus, full accrual basis
questions.explain: Government-wide statements use the economic resources measurement focus and the full accrual basis (like a business), reporting capital assets and long-term debt. Governmental funds, by contrast, use the current financial resources focus and modified accrual.

questions.q: For Selk, a nongovernmental not-for-profit organization, donated services is treated as follows:
choices: Recognized for all volunteer hours at fair value
Never recognized
Recognized only if they create or enhance a nonfinancial asset, or require specialized skills that would otherwise be purchased
Recognized only if the donor requests it
questions.explain: Donated services are recognized only if they create or enhance a nonfinancial asset, or require specialized skills (for example an attorney, CPA, or electrician) that would otherwise have to be purchased. General volunteer hours that fail both tests are not recognized.

questions.q: For Tove, a nongovernmental not-for-profit organization, a quid pro quo contribution where the donor receives a benefit is treated as follows:
choices: Contribution revenue equals the full amount paid
Contribution revenue equals the fair value of the benefit
No contribution revenue is recognized
Contribution revenue equals the amount paid minus the fair value of the benefit received
questions.explain: A quid pro quo payment is part exchange and part gift. Contribution revenue equals the amount paid minus the fair value of the benefit the donor receives; the exchange portion is recognized under the exchange (ASC 606) model. Recording the full amount as contribution overstates the gift.

questions.q: For Urne, a nongovernmental not-for-profit organization, a cost-reimbursement grant is treated as follows:
choices: Conditional — revenue is recognized as qualifying costs are incurred
Unconditional — revenue is recognized immediately
Recognized only when the grant is closed out
Recorded as a liability until the grant expires
questions.explain: A cost-reimbursement grant contains a barrier (incurring qualifying costs) and a right of return, which makes it a conditional contribution: recognize revenue only as the qualifying costs are incurred. It is not unconditional, you do not wait for close-out, and you do not park the entire amount as a liability indefinitely.

questions.q: For Vance, a nongovernmental not-for-profit organization, an unconditional pledge collectible over several years is treated as follows:
choices: Deferred until the cash is received
Contribution revenue now at present value, net of an estimated uncollectible allowance
Recognized only as each installment arrives
Recorded as a conditional promise
questions.explain: An unconditional pledge is recognized as contribution revenue now, measured at the present value of the expected future cash flows and net of an allowance for uncollectible amounts; multi-year pledges are generally donor-restricted by time. You do not defer to cash receipt and you do not treat it as a conditional promise.

questions.q: For Pard, a nongovernmental not-for-profit organization, the net asset classes a nongovernmental NFP reports is treated as follows:
choices: Unrestricted, temporarily restricted, permanently restricted
Restricted and unrestricted fund balances
With donor restrictions and without donor restrictions
Spendable and nonspendable
questions.explain: Under current GAAP an NFP reports two net asset classes: with donor restrictions and without donor restrictions. The older three-bucket model (unrestricted, temporarily restricted, permanently restricted) was superseded, and governmental fund-balance terms do not apply to NFPs.

questions.q: In partnership accounting, a noncash asset contributed by a partner is handled as:
choices: Recorded at the partner's carrying amount
Recorded at its fair value at the contribution date
Recorded at original cost
Recorded at the lower of cost or fair value
questions.explain: Noncash assets contributed to a partnership are recorded at fair value on the contribution date, with the contributing partner's capital account credited for that fair value. The partner's old carrying amount, original cost, or lower-of-cost-or-fair-value are not used, because each partner's capital should reflect the economic value brought into the partnership.

questions.q: In partnership accounting, the order of payment in a partnership liquidation is handled as:
choices: Partner capital, then loans, then creditors
Pro rata among all claimants
Outside creditors, then partner loans, then partner capital
Partner loans, then creditors, then capital
questions.explain: Liquidation priority is outside (third-party) creditors first, then partner loans, then partner capital balances. Outside claimants are satisfied before partners recover anything they lent to or invested in the partnership.

questions.q: In partnership accounting, the bonus method when an incoming partner pays more than book value for an interest is handled as:
choices: Goodwill is recorded for the excess
The excess is recognized as income
The excess is recorded as a liability
The excess is allocated to the existing partners' capital; no goodwill is recorded
questions.explain: Under the bonus method, total recorded capital equals the actual assets invested, so any premium the incoming partner pays above book value is shifted to the existing partners' capital accounts as a bonus. No goodwill (an unrecorded intangible) is created, that would be the goodwill method, which is generally disfavored.

questions.q: In partnership accounting, allocation of partnership profit when salary and interest allowances exceed net income is handled as:
choices: Allowances are still allocated, and the resulting deficiency is shared in the loss ratio
No allowances are given when income is insufficient
Income is split equally regardless of the agreement
Allowances are prorated to available income
questions.explain: Salary and interest allowances are allocation devices, not expenses, so they are applied in full even when they exceed net income. The resulting negative residual is then divided among the partners in their profit-and-loss (residual) ratio. Income is not simply split equally or prorated to available income when the agreement specifies allowances.

questions.q: Frye Company (the parent) acquired 80% of a subsidiary. Consideration transferred was $600,000, the fair value of the noncontrolling interest was $140,000, and the fair value of the subsidiary's identifiable net assets was $700,000. What is goodwill?
questions.explain: Goodwill = consideration + FV of NCI − FV of identifiable net assets = $600,000 + $140,000 − $700,000 = $40,000. Distractors omit the NCI, use only the parent's share of net assets, or add net assets instead of subtracting.

questions.q: Goss Company (the parent) acquired 75% of a subsidiary. Consideration transferred was $600,000, the fair value of the noncontrolling interest was $175,000, and the fair value of the subsidiary's identifiable net assets was $700,000. What is goodwill?
questions.explain: Goodwill = consideration + FV of NCI − FV of identifiable net assets = $600,000 + $175,000 − $700,000 = $75,000. Distractors omit the NCI, use only the parent's share of net assets, or add net assets instead of subtracting.

questions.q: Hale Company (the parent) acquired 70% of a subsidiary. Consideration transferred was $600,000, the fair value of the noncontrolling interest was $210,000, and the fair value of the subsidiary's identifiable net assets was $700,000. What is goodwill?
questions.explain: Goodwill = consideration + FV of NCI − FV of identifiable net assets = $600,000 + $210,000 − $700,000 = $110,000. Distractors omit the NCI, use only the parent's share of net assets, or add net assets instead of subtracting.

questions.q: Ives Company (the parent) acquired 70% of a subsidiary. Consideration transferred was $600,000, the fair value of the noncontrolling interest was $240,000, and the fair value of the subsidiary's identifiable net assets was $800,000. What is goodwill?

How much interest should be capitalized this year?
questions.explain: Capitalized interest = weighted-average accumulated expenditures × rate = $400,000 × 6% = $24,000 (capped at actual interest, not binding here). Distractors apply the rate to total cost, omit the rate, or halve the result.
